subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s Degree from an accredited university
Completed degree in Photography, Visual Arts, or a related field
Received hands-on training in digital photography and editing during undergraduate studies
Participated in workshops on newborn and family photography techniques
Undertook continuing education courses in digital editing and lighting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: In-Hospital Photographer for Bella Baby
Responsible for photographing newborns and families within 24-48 hours of birth
Manages all aspects of on-site photo shoots, editing, and sales presentations
Acts as the primary point of contact for parents during the photography process

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the professional photography sector
Operates at the intersection of healthcare and consumer services
Specializes in in-hospital newborn and family portraiture
Part of the broader creative and visual arts industry

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed by Bella Baby, a medium-sized company specializing in newborn photography
Works primarily within hospital environments as a contractor or direct employee
Often operates independently onsite, coordinating with hospital staff as needed
Collaborates with other photographers and sales staff remotely

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Business segment: On-location family and newborn photography
Focuses on providing high-quality photography services to new parents in hospitals
Operates in a business-to-consumer (B2C) model
Targets parents seeking to capture their baby's first moments professionally

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 6-10 years of experience as a professional photographer
Began working in-hospital newborn photography around age 36-40
Maintains a consistent client-facing role throughout tenure
Has adapted to new technology and workflow changes over the years

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Mid- to senior-level photographer based on years of experience
Acts as the lead on all assigned in-hospital photo sessions
Possesses advanced skills in both photography and client communication
Occasionally mentors newer photographers or shares best practices

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Surveys hospital rooms to identify newborns within the optimal photography window
Communicates with parents to offer and schedule photography sessions
Conducts 30-minute in-room photo shoots using professional camera equipment
Edits and curates photos onsite using Adobe Lightroom Classic
Presents edited galleries and walks parents through package options

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends about 50% of work time on photography sessions (shooting and setup)
Dedicates approximately 30% of time to photo editing and gallery preparation
Uses around 20% of work time presenting galleries and processing orders
Initial room surveys and scheduling take a small but critical daily portion

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Works independently within hospital guidelines
Makes real-time decisions about photo composition, lighting, and editing
Determines which photos to include in the final gallery for each family
Has discretion in how to present and upsell product packages to clients

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Interacting empathetically with new parents to build trust
Operating the Sony a7 II camera for newborn portraiture
Quickly identifying the best angles and lighting in challenging hospital rooms
Editing photos for standard corrections such as exposure and white balance

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Managing photo shoots with fussy or uncooperative newborns
Editing out distracting background elements without losing image integrity
Balancing time constraints when multiple families request sessions simultaneously
Handling emotional or sensitive situations with families in postpartum recovery

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Initial room surveys and scheduling could benefit from automated hospital record integration
Culling through large numbers of similar photos to select the best 18-20 shots
Standard exposure and blemish corrections during editing
Processing orders and managing client data entry on the iPad

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Finds the creative aspect of composing and shooting newborn portraits highly enjoyable
Enjoys connecting with families during gallery reveals and seeing emotional reactions
Considers repetitive order entry and paperwork to be tedious
Editing is enjoyable when it involves creative problem-solving, but routine corrections can feel monotonous

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Limited space and variable lighting in hospital rooms often slow down setup
Manual entry of client information on the iPad can be repetitive and error-prone
Occasional delays due to waiting for parental consent or room availability
Time pressure to edit and deliver galleries quickly can be stressful

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Higher enjoyment and skill in photography lead to increased session efficiency and better results
Productivity peaks when engaged in creative or client-facing tasks
Tedious or repetitive tasks tend to slow down workflow and reduce overall satisfaction
Learning new editing techniques or AI tools can reinvigorate interest and boost output

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Generally positive and curious about new tech that enhances workflow
Views technology as a necessary evolution in professional photography
Actively seeks out tools that improve efficiency or image quality
Open to experimenting with new AI-based editing features

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Considers self highly adaptable due to regular adoption of new apps and editing tools
Quickly learned to use generative AI features in Adobe Lightroom
Comfortable integrating LLM-based tools into daily editing and communication tasks
Able to troubleshoot and learn new devices (e.g., iPad, MacBook Pro) with minimal support

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate personal time to online tutorials or webinars for new tools
Will invest more time if the technology directly impacts photo quality or client experience
Prefers hands-on learning and short, focused training sessions
Less likely to engage in formal, lengthy courses unless required

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by desire to streamline workflow and deliver higher quality images
Workload and tight daily schedules can be a barrier to learning new systems
Relevance to daily tasks is a key factor in prioritizing what to learn
Peer recommendations or company mandates increase motivation to adopt new tech

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers sharing success stories with new tools encourages experimentation
Management's endorsement of generative AI features led to early adoption
Occasionally participates in informal group learning or knowledge sharing
Company-provided iPads and software influence choice of workflow tools

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Adobe Lightroom Classic for photo editing
Processes orders and displays galleries using a company-issued iPad
Utilizes generative AI features within Lightroom for background extension and object removal
Relies on internal Bella Baby software for client management and consent forms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Sony a7 II camera paired with Sigma 24-70mm f2.8 lens for image capture
M1 MacBook Pro as primary editing workstation
Printed materials for package explanations and consent information
Standard hospital room props and linens used as photo backgrounds

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient with DSLRs and mirrorless cameras from major brands (Sony, Canon, Nikon)
Fluent in using Adobe Creative Cloud suite, especially Lightroom and Photoshop
Familiar with electronic consent and client management systems common in photography businesses
Knowledgeable about modern photo printing and package delivery systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasional challenges transferring RAW files from Sony camera to MacBook Pro
Limited integration between iPad order system and desktop editing software
Needs to manually sync edited galleries from Lightroom to iPad for client viewing
Some friction when moving between digital and printed materials during client presentations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic concepts of machine learning, generative AI, and large language models
Familiar with terms like 'generative fill,' 'object removal,' and 'prompting'
Recognizes the role of AI in automating repetitive editing tasks
Has learned AI terminology through online resources and industry updates

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses generative AI features in Adobe Lightroom Classic for editing
Leverages LLM-based tools for text generation and communication tasks when needed
Has experimented with AI upscaling and background removal plugins
Occasionally tests new AI photo-editing apps as they become available

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Applies AI tools weekly, primarily during the photo editing process
Uses AI to expand backgrounds, remove distracting objects, and retouch blemishes
Employs LLMs to draft client communications or captions for galleries
AI tools help maintain a consistent, professional look across all client galleries

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI successfully removes medical equipment and clutter from hospital room photos
Occasionally, AI-generated backgrounds may look unnatural and require manual correction
Learned to always review AI-edited images closely before sharing with clients
Noted that generative AI speeds up routine edits but cannot replace creative decision-making

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Company provides basic onboarding for iPad and editing software, including AI features
Peers often share tips and AI editing tricks via online forums or group chats
Access to vendor-provided tutorials for new generative AI tools in Lightroom
Occasionally receives update emails or webinars introducing new AI functionality

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers independent use of AI tools with manual review of outputs
Occasionally employs step-by-step collaboration when testing new AI features
Enjoys the flexibility of toggling between automated and manual editing modes
Likes to maintain creative control but welcomes AI assistance for routine tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI as a digital assistant, primarily for repetitive or technical edits
Occasionally treats AI as a creative advisor for background extensions
Maintains final decision-making authority over all edits and gallery selections
Does not fully delegate any critical task to AI without human oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to incorporating new AI-driven workflows if they save time or improve quality
Willing to experiment with AI features in beta or early-release stages
Appreciates incremental workflow changes rather than radical overhauls
Prefers clear documentation and support when piloting new AI tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Leans toward command-based or menu-driven AI interfaces for editing tasks
Finds conversational interfaces helpful for brainstorming or learning new features
Uses prompt-based workflows in LLMs for drafting text or communication
Prefers simple, intuitive controls without excessive back-and-forth

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Photo composition and newborn handling require nuanced, experience-based judgment
Editing for emotional impact and storytelling is highly subjective
Client interactions depend on empathy and social sensitivity
AI tools supplement but do not replace domain expertise in newborn photography

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for routine tasks like object removal or background extension, with manual review
Does not rely on AI for final selection of gallery images
Always double-checks AI-edited photos before showing to clients
Skeptical of fully-automated editing without human input

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to handle technical, repetitive aspects of editing
Believes creative choices and final approvals should remain human-led
Supports a hybrid workflow where AI augments—not replaces—human skill
Values the ability to override or fine-tune AI outputs easily

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for tasks without creative or emotional impact (e.g., bulk blemish removal)
Finds automation threatening if it risks homogenizing artistic style or client experience
Desires transparency and control over any fully-automated processes
Wants clear opt-out options for features that might overstep creative boundaries

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances technical skills by allowing more time for creativity
Thinks core skills like composition and client interaction are irreplaceable
Views AI as a tool to refine, not diminish, photographic craftsmanship
Sees potential risk to purely technical editing skills if over-automated

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing proficiency in AI prompt engineering for editing tasks
Learning to review, critique, and correct AI-generated edits
Staying updated on latest AI tools and industry best practices
Advising peers on integrating AI efficiently into their workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mild concern about AI automating large portions of editing over time
Confident that personal interaction and creative vision keep the role secure
Believes fully-automated newborn photography is unlikely to replace human photographers soon
Keeps an eye on industry trends for early signs of disruptive automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: All AI edits are manually reviewed and can be reverted in Lightroom
Retains full editing history to undo unwanted AI changes
Company policy encourages human oversight for any AI-altered imagery
Can disable or bypass AI features if unsatisfied with results

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company has recently encouraged adoption of generative AI features for editing
Policies emphasize AI as a productivity tool, not a replacement for photographers
Workshops and guides have been introduced to promote responsible AI use
Team discussions now routinely include best practices for AI integration

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will continue to transform creative industries by automating repetitive tasks
Sees potential for AI to democratize access to high-quality photography
Cautiously optimistic about AI's ability to enhance, rather than replace, human artistry
Recognizes ethical challenges in authenticity and originality with AI-generated content

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports transparent disclosure of AI-edited images to clients
Worried about over-reliance on AI leading to loss of creative skills
Believes ethical boundaries must be set regarding image manipulation
Advocates for clear consent from clients when AI significantly alters photos

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires AI that can automatically adjust to variable hospital lighting conditions
Would benefit from AI that can suggest or automate optimal photo culling
Wishes for seamless integration between editing software and client gallery platforms
Hopes for AI-driven scheduling tools to streamline daily room surveys

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Anticipates more AI-driven editing features becoming standard in photo workflows
Expects client communication and scheduling to become increasingly automated
Believes creative and interpersonal aspects will remain central to the role
Foresees the need to continually update skills to stay ahead of AI advancements

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Invest in ongoing training for staff on new AI tools and ethical guidelines
Solicit feedback from photographers to guide AI feature development
Implement clear policies on client consent and disclosure for AI-edited images
Pilot AI-driven workflow enhancements before full-scale rollouts
